name: knowledge-base

applies_to.description: Topic-organized markdown wiki or knowledge base. No code expected. Structure: each top-level directory is a topic, with its own README.md and subtopic subdirectories. The project is navigated by directory hierarchy rather than a build process. Common tools: Obsidian, MkDocs, plain git + markdown. Authorial judgment on topic placement is high — ORGANIZE is ASK_USER-heavy by design for this template.

default_rules.note: Structural root files (README, INDEX, LICENSE, CHANGELOG) are canonically at project root and must not be moved; these files orient readers to the knowledge base
Per-topic README files are at their canonical location; each topic directory should have its own README.md that explains the topic's scope and contents
Markdown files already placed inside topic subtrees are at their canonical location; ORGANIZE does not second-guess topic placement for files that are already within a topic directory
Draft articles under drafts/ are at their canonical location if the project maintains a drafts/ area; applies only if drafts/ exists — do not create it speculatively
Temporary scratch markdown files are candidates for deletion; quarantine to .delete — always ASK_USER before ratifying, as knowledge-base authors sometimes use scratch files as active working notes

Numbered note files (notes1.md, notes22.md) — ASK_USER; likely .archive candidates, but in a knowledge base these may be intentional chronological notes that the author wants to keep accessible; if this pattern recurs across many files, FLAG_NEW_RULE to establish a consistent routing

Loose markdown at project root not matched by earlier rules — ASK_USER always; could belong in a topic directory, could be a historical file, or could be an intentionally root-level orientation document; topic placement is authorial and cannot be determined from the filename alone

notes: Knowledge base template. This template is ASK_USER-heavy by design — topic placement is authorial and cannot be reliably automated. The hierarchy of a knowledge base reflects the author's mental model of the domain; ORGANIZE should surface ambiguity and let the author decide, not impose a structure. Use FLAG_NEW_RULE to promote recurring patterns to explicit rules over time (e.g., if the author consistently puts meeting notes in a meetings/ directory, a rule for that pattern should be promoted). The .obsidian/** ignore path covers Obsidian vault metadata (themes, plugins, workspace state) — do not touch this. Non-markdown files (images, PDFs embedded in articles) should be handled by adding project-specific rules during BOOTSTRAP or via FLAG_NEW_RULE once the pattern is observed.
